title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: The Science of Memory and Recall
content.text: Every time you remember something, you are performing a remarkable feat of mental reconstruction. Memory is not a simple recording device that plays back events in perfect detail. Instead, it is an active, creative process that can surprise even scientists who study it. (37) [37] Researchers have shown that memories are rebuilt from fragments each time they are retrieved. [/37] This means that what we remember is often shaped as much by our present state of mind as by what actually happened. || The brain stores memories through a complex network of neurons. When we experience something new, neurons fire together and form connections. (38) [38] This is why repeating information helps it stick, and why cramming the night before an exam is far less effective than spreading study over several sessions. [/38] Over time, memories that are revisited frequently become stronger, while those left untouched gradually fade. || Not all memories are equal. Some experiences leave vivid, almost photographic impressions on the mind, particularly those linked to strong emotions. (39) [39] Psychologists believe this heightened encoding is an evolutionary survival mechanism. [/39] We are wired to remember events that threatened or delighted us because such memories may protect us in the future. || Sleep plays a surprisingly important role in consolidating memories. During the night, the brain processes and organises the information it gathered during the day. (40) [40] Students who slept after studying performed significantly better on tests than those who stayed awake. [/40] Even a short nap has been shown to improve recall of recently learned material. || Forgetting, despite its frustrating reputation, is not simply a failure of the memory system. It is, in many ways, a feature. (41) [41] Without the ability to discard irrelevant detail, the mind would quickly become overwhelmed. [/41] The brain filters out information it deems unnecessary, keeping our thinking clear and focused. || Researchers are now exploring ways to enhance memory using technology. Apps that use spaced repetition remind users to review material at intervals precisely calculated to prevent forgetting. (42) [42] However, many experts caution that understanding memory's natural mechanisms remains the best foundation for learning. [/42]

paragraphs.A: Experiments have confirmed this link between rest and retention.
paragraphs.B: A selective memory is, paradoxically, a healthy one.
paragraphs.C: A shocking accident or a moment of great joy, for instance, tends to be recalled with unusual clarity.
paragraphs.D: The stronger those connections, the more reliably the memory can be retrieved later.
paragraphs.E: These digital tools are already transforming how students around the world prepare for exams.
paragraphs.F: Far from being a fixed archive, memory is more like a story that gets rewritten with every telling.
paragraphs.G: Some scientists argue that the very act of remembering changes the memory itself.

questions.explanation: F introduces the idea that memory is an active and changing process, anticipating the explanation that follows.
D directly explains the consequence of the neural connections mentioned in the previous sentence.
C gives concrete examples of intense emotional experiences, fitting with the previous statement about vivid memories.
A introduces experimental evidence on sleep and retention, connecting with the argument in the paragraph.
B summarises the paradoxical idea of forgetting as a positive feature, consistent with the tone of the paragraph.
E describes the current impact of these digital tools, complementing the mention of spaced repetition apps.
